assertions.title: Graph-first default landing exposes investor-path entry without a separate proof-page handoff

steps.action: Open the root route at http://127.0.0.1:3100 with a desktop viewport.
steps.expected: The default landing mode should already be a graph-first workspace with the investor-path entry visible in the same surface.
steps.observed: The initial viewport loaded on the BETTER Atlas workspace with the node grid, map row, overview control, and Investor Pitch Path affordance visible before the hero/proof section, which remained below the fold.

steps.action: Inspect the primary graph workspace controls before any scrolling or route changes.
steps.expected: Users should be able to see a visible investor-path start affordance directly inside the graph workspace.
steps.observed: Both the prominent 'Investor Pitch Path' button and the secondary 'start the investor path' affordance were present inside the root graph workspace.

steps.action: Click the graph-workspace Investor Pitch Path control.
steps.expected: The guided path should begin immediately inside the same graph workspace without a separate proof-page handoff.
steps.observed: Clicking 'Investor Pitch Path' entered gate 1/8 in-place, keeping the graph cards and atlas context on screen rather than routing to a different landing/proof page.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server
evidence.network: No failing requests observed; entering the investor path was an in-page graph state change rather than a separate proof-page navigation.

assertions.title: The default loaded exploration state is a pure graph workspace

steps.action: Load the root route and inspect the first desktop viewport.
steps.expected: Core exploration should default to a graph workspace rather than a proof/shell handoff, with the graph as the primary visible surface.
steps.observed: The first viewport was the graph workspace: BETTER Atlas heading, node grid, overview control, persistent map row, and investor-path entry were all visible immediately; the hero/proof composition appeared only below the fold.

steps.action: Inspect orientation recovery and graph-position cues in the workspace.
steps.expected: A persistent minimap or orientation cue should remain visible so users can recover position inside the graph.
steps.observed: The workspace kept a visible orientation frame via the overview button plus the persistent MAP/minimap row listing all atlas nodes while the graph remained onscreen.

steps.action: Enter the guided path and then switch to Roadmap / Back to overview inside the workspace.
steps.expected: A sticky inspector or equivalent detail surface should remain available alongside the graph, and direct node traversal should stay inside the workspace.
steps.observed: Starting the path converted the lower inspector into a persistent detail surface while the graph cards stayed visible above it, and the recorded Roadmap → Back to overview flow stayed entirely inside the workspace with direct node traversal controls.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server
evidence.network: No failing requests observed; tested workspace traversals were local graph/hash state transitions.

assertions.title: The graph exposes a coherent VC-friendly pitch path with enforced gate ordering

steps.action: Start the Investor Pitch Path from the graph workspace.
steps.expected: The graph should expose the ordered gates problem → wedge → proof → moat → business model → roadmap gates → valuation → appendix/evidence inside one continuous path.
steps.observed: Gate chips and the gate counter rendered the full ordered sequence in one strip: Problem, Wedge, Proof, Moat, Business Model, Roadmap Gates, Valuation, Appendix / Evidence.

steps.action: Advance through the guided path using the next-gate controls.
steps.expected: Traversal should stay in the same graph workspace while progressing through the ordered pitch gates without disconnected hunting.
steps.observed: A single recording successfully advanced through all eight gates in order inside the same workspace, with each gate keeping the graph cards, map cues, and gate-strip context visible.

steps.action: Inspect the path at the Valuation and Appendix / Evidence gates.
steps.expected: Valuation and Appendix / Evidence should appear as ordered late-path gates rather than disconnected destinations.
steps.observed: The Valuation view appeared as gate 7/8 with the ordered strip intact, and clicking Appendix / Evidence moved directly to the Evidence & Sources node as gate 8/8 in the same sequence.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server
evidence.network: No failing requests observed; the guided pitch traversal used in-page graph state changes.

assertions.title: Every primary roadmap stage has its own execution-plan treatment with falsifiable proof gates and bounded timing windows

steps.action: Open the guided path's Roadmap Gates stage and inspect the default roadmap-stage viewport.
steps.expected: Each visible primary roadmap stage should surface its own execution-plan treatment with workstreams, external dependencies, falsifiable public proof gates, and bounded timing windows.
steps.observed: The Roadmap Gates stage rendered roadmap cards with maturity labels, descriptions, caveats, and 'Depends on' text, but no visible execution-plan sections, workstream lists, proof-gate framing, or bounded timing windows.

steps.action: Scroll farther down within the same roadmap-stage view.
steps.expected: Lower in the same view, users should still be able to find the dedicated execution-plan treatment for the visible stages.
steps.observed: Further scrolling revealed the interactive roadmap atlas branch-family list and counts, but still no per-stage execution-plan content for the stages visible in this flow.

steps.action: Check the rendered page text for execution-plan signals.
steps.expected: Execution-plan copy should expose terms/structures equivalent to workstreams, external dependencies, proof gates, and bounded timing windows.
steps.observed: Browser text inspection found no rendered 'Execution plan', 'Workstreams', 'External dependencies', 'Proof gates', 'Timing window', 'weeks after', or 'Q3 2026' strings in the tested Roadmap Gates surface.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server
evidence.network: No failing requests observed; the failure was visible in rendered content rather than network behavior.
observations: The visible roadmap-stage cards only exposed summary, caveat, and dependency snippets.
The lower-scroll roadmap atlas showed branch families and node counts, not execution-plan panels.
The tested surface did not expose falsifiable public proof gates or bounded timing windows for the visible stages.

assertions.issues: VAL-ROADMAP-016 failed because the Roadmap Gates surface still reads as summary cards plus branch listings, not as dedicated per-stage execution plans with explicit workstreams, external dependencies, falsifiable public proof gates, and bounded timing windows.

assertions.title: Guided investor path is visibly discoverable and resumable inside the graph workspace

steps.action: Inspect the default graph workspace before entering the guided path.
steps.expected: A clearly visible investor-path entry affordance should exist inside the graph workspace.
steps.observed: The root graph workspace exposed a prominent 'Investor Pitch Path' control plus a secondary 'start the investor path' affordance.

steps.action: Advance to gate 3 and inspect progress UI.
steps.expected: The path should show progress or return cues so users can tell where they are in the sequence.
steps.observed: At Proof & Trust, the workspace showed 'INVESTOR PATH — GATE 3/8', a '3 OF 8' counter, and the full gate-chip strip with the current gate highlighted.

steps.action: Leave the path for a detour and then return.
steps.expected: After a detour, users should be able to resume the last visited gate rather than restarting from the beginning.
steps.observed: Detouring to Risks & Caveats surfaced a 'Resume at “Proof”' button, and the rerun recording showed that clicking it returned the user directly to the Proof gate state.

evidence.consoleErrors: none; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server
evidence.network: No failing requests observed; detour/resume behavior was handled inside the graph workspace.

frictions.description: Several controls share the same accessible names across graph cards and minimap rows (for example 'Roadmap' and 'Risks & Caveats'), which caused strict-locator collisions during automation.
frictions.resolution: Switched to explicit element refs or reran the capture from a state where the intended control was unambiguous; final evidence uses the successful reruns.

frictions.description: The secondary footer affordance 'start the investor path' timed out when clicked by ref, and a first resume recording could not be overwritten after a partial strict-mode failure.
frictions.resolution: Used the primary 'Investor Pitch Path' control for the stable path-entry flow and saved the successful detour/resume rerun as `VAL-ROADMAP-017-investor-path-resume-after-detour-v2.webm`.

summary: Tested 5 assertions: 4 passed and 1 failed. VAL-CROSS-014, VAL-ROADMAP-014, VAL-ROADMAP-015, and VAL-ROADMAP-017 passed: the root loads as a graph-first atlas, investor-path entry is visible directly in that workspace, the guided pitch path stays ordered through valuation and appendix/evidence, and detour/resume works with visible progress cues. VAL-ROADMAP-016 failed because the Roadmap Gates surface still renders summary cards and branch listings without visible per-stage execution-plan workstreams, falsifiable public proof gates, or bounded timing windows. No browser runtime errors were observed; console output only showed repeated React DevTools info banners and [HMR] connected logs from the local Next.js dev server.
